All right, welcome back to your next lesson on prospecting. Listen, thanks for taking that last quiz, doing the exercises. This stuff is crucial. Remember, everything builds on the thing that came before it, so we're making great progress. Let's move on now to the third rule of straight line, prospecting, which is you always want to use a script for prospecting. In other words, don't wing it. There's always going to be your best set of questions that you want to ask people for your industry. Now, I'll get to some big picture questions later on, but just so you understand, in your specific industry, there's going to be the best group of well thought out questions, and those questions are going to need to be asked in a certain order. It's not random, okay? And we'll go through that later again. What the point is, is that you have two ways to go about doing that. One, you can try to remember everything, say, I'm going to remember all these questions and say them in exactly the right order, or you could engage in what I call strategic preparation and prepare yourself beforehand, map out the entire process of gathering intelligence, write out all your questions, all the potential follow-up questions, do that in the best order possible. And that serves the outcome, which is to find out what your prospects needs are and so forth, right? And it makes you sound or continue to sound like the true expert in your field who knows exactly what he has to say before he says it, and you're in this sort of guided intelligence gathering phrase that's what I call elegant. See, if you don't write your questions down, you tend to go, you're like, it's like a ping pong ball, right? You're like, so, you know, what area did you come from? Let's say it's real estate, right? See, so where are you moving from? The guy says, X, Y, Z, okay, and how old are your kids? It's like, what? Like, you didn't say, do you have kids? Or what schools do they go to? It's a logical way to ask questions. And even then, I didn't script this out, because I don't have a teleprompter. I'm just talking off top of my head to you guys, right? So the point is just to make it up. I don't know what to say first. As good as I am with my tongue, right? But if you gave me time, I could script out the absolute perfect series of questions to ask someone in the real estate industry and memorize them. So I'm not saying you want to have them in front of you. Where did you come from? That would be stupid. But you memorize the questions, you put them in the perfect order, and by doing that, when you have a script, and we'll get to this a lot more when it comes to presenting a different module, right? But when you have that script, it does so many other things. You see, when you know what you're going to say before you say it, it then frees you up now to actually be looking at your prospect and looking for visual cues and their ahums and ums, their body language, their tonality, what they say. So you'll focus more on your prospect and listening to their answers than actively trying to remember and pull these questions from out of left field. So when you have a script and you know what you're going to say before you say it, it makes you much better a listener. And that's the key to prospecting. You want to be an effective listener. And with that, let's now move on to your fourth rule of straight line prospecting, which is you want to go from less invasive questions to more invasive questions. And this is a really, really important one. For instance, if you start off straight away and the first question out of your mouth starts prying into their financial condition, oh boy, good luck trying to close that person. If they're over the phone, they'll click Hang Up on your fear in person. They'll probably give you that and walk out the door. People do not like to have their inner private life invaded with direct questions about their finances from a stranger, which you are when you first meet a prospect, right? Very different for repeat business. But this is about prospecting with people who you don't know. So just imagine if you are a stockbroker, for instance, or an insurance broker or anything else. It's just a matter of what you have. And you get someone on the phone, you say a couple quick questions so I don't waste your time. How much money are you liquid for right now to invest in a stock? Click Hello. Hello. Mr. Jones, look, where are you? He's gone. He's like, screw you. Excuse me? You want to know how much money I have in the bank? You don't know me. Never will answer the question. Never. I mean, literally never, right? But here's the insane part of the weird part, right? Even nature as it may be or as it is, right? If you start off by asking questions that are the sort of questions that anybody would answer and then you slowly start to dig deeper within a minute and a half or two, they'll be telling you how much money they have in the bank, how much they put into a particular idea in terms of a stock, right? How many other brokers are dealing with how much money they have in the market so you'd start off by saying something. No. Typically, what type of stocks do you like? Growth or income? And when you consider yourself an aggressive investor or a more conservative investor, uh-huh, forget the listening part. We'll go through that separately here. But you see how those are basically questions that are innocuous. Anyone would answer those questions. And then slowly but surely, as you start peeling back those layers, layer by layer, next thing you know, you're saying, oh, and typically when you find a stock at your life, how much would you invest in that? Just the ballpark. No big deal totality. And they'll tell you because you've already asked them three or four other less invasive questions. And then lastly, say in the ballpark, what do you have in the market right now? Just you know, between all your brokerage accounts, give me just an estimate and they will answer you almost all of the time because you started off with those less invasive questions and then you went through the more invasive questions after you built up more money in the rapport bank, so to speak, by getting into rapport through asking questions. All right. Now, next, you must always follow a logical path. Now, I mentioned this before a couple of times here, but I want to reiterate it and just kind of point it out as a rule to itself here. And what that means just to say it again is that you don't want to just ping pong all over the place and ask one question about one subject without exhausting the line of questioning because there might be follow up questions based on what your prospect says, right? So you want to ask a question, follow that logical path, exhaust the line of questioning as opposed to jumping from this to that to that in the process like ping pong ball, what the hell? One second you're talking about his children, next second you're talking about when he doesn't work, then you're talking about his life when he's hanging out and watching sports. It makes no sense. Here's the deal. This is the best metaphor. There are some doctors who have been trained correctly, right? You go to medical school and they have a decent bedside manner. When they ask you questions, what do they do? They start off, they go through a complete history. They follow a logical path. What are the basic amulets? You have to start when you're younger and they get more and more specific, big picture to small picture, less invasive to more invasive. They're logical the whole way through. As soon as you start going into an illogical routine, you know what they say? Novice alert. Novice alert. You reveal yourself as not being an expert. It's one of those rules that you must adhere to because when you go off of that path, that straight line, so to speak, it reveals that you're not truly an expert. Remember what I said. It's not just those first four seconds. That first four seconds captures their imagination. It starts off the process of you being perceived the right way. That's an emotional reaction they have. But after that, the words that you say, the logical path that you follow, all the other things will make sure that they can teach you to not only feel that way, but say, wow, I was correct to perceive the person this way. They truly are an expert. They really know what they're doing. They can help me get what I want. They can help me resolve my pain and so forth. That's why you got to follow the logical path. And that leads me to the next lesson for straight line prospecting, which is that as you ask your questions, you want to ask them in a certain tonality. See, every question must be accompanied by a specific tonality. And if that tonality is the right tonality, it opens someone up to staying or even doing building more rapport with you. And if you ask it in the wrong tonality, boom, your break for rapport complete. Let me give you an example. Let's say the first question you asked someone is, so how long have you been living in the area for? When did you first move here? So let's say you say something. So when did you first move here? They're implicitly saying, well, this person doesn't really care about me. They just asked me they couldn't care if I live or die basically. Or you say, so tell me, how long have you been living in the area for? I really want to know tonality. You know, this person is really genuinely interested in me. And you open them up to answering and you build rapport. See the difference? So one way would be, so how long are you living in the area for? And they're saying, you know that I know that I know that you know that I don't really care about that. I'm just peeling back the ladies here and being polite so I can then ram my product on your throat. That's really what you're saying, by the way, is I don't really care about you. But when you ask the question the right way, so tell me, how long have you been living in the area for? They're like, oh, well, he really cares. Remember your rules from tonality, right? And getting into rapport. Repour is based on what two things. Number one, you care. You're not just there to close or you care about them. And two, you're just like them. You process things the same way. You understand the world the same way, right? Those are the basics of rapport. So when you come up with the wrong tonality, you're done. They just, they know you don't care. Okay? Let me give you another example. One important one. When you say to someone, when you ask them a question about identifying their pain, so tell me what's your biggest worry right now? What's really keeping you up at night? They're like, screw you. You say, what's really keeping you up at night? They're like, huh, what do you say? So tell me, what's your biggest worry now? What's really keeping you up at night? What's really, what's, is that one thing, what is that's really making you nervous right now? So tell me, imagine you're an insurance sales person and you ask them what a question about what's really making them worried right now about not having the appropriate coverage in place. And you say, so tell me, you know, what is it that at the end of the day, what's that one thing that if, you know, if we could solve it with insurance, what would that be? You know, if God was telling you, what would that one thing that really keeps you up at night? And they'll be like, oh my God, this person really cares about me. They can feel my pain because you use the sympathy, empathy. I feel your pain tonality. And bam, just like that, you've built massive rapport. The other way you actually just shatter rapport. So imagine this. So tell me, what's your biggest pain right now? What's really making you suffer at night right now? What's hurting your business most? Well, what's hurting your business most right now? It's a very, very different, you know, ball of wax the way those two. So let me give you a question. I'm gonna give you two examples here. Let's do this just once here. And there'll be a lot more in the back end when we go, when you go through the, the quizzes here, right? Let me just give you one to make sure you got this concept and it's locked in, right? So I'm gonna ask you a question about pain here. And you tell me which one is better or worse, the first one or the second one? Here's the first one. So tell me, what's your biggest headache right now when it comes to your business? What's really keeping you up at night? That's number one. And now here comes number two. Okay. So what's your biggest pain right now in your business? What is really keeping you? What's really hurting you right now? It's not just making money. Which one is it? Now which of the two is the first one better than one or the second one? Obviously the first one, correct, right? In my tonality, I'm telling them that I care. I'm here to help them. I resolving their pain. You get it? So you actually set yourself up for greatness. Really? So you think the second one is gonna actually get you somewhere? Let me be frank with you. If you come at somebody with an aggressive tonality, when it comes to their pain, I'm just telling you, you're gonna break all rapport and you have absolutely positively no shot of closing the deal. In my tonality, I'm telling them that I care. I'm here to help them. I resolving their pain. You get it? So you actually set yourself up for greatness. It's a crucial thing here, okay? And again, just remember the most important thing is that when you're asking these invasive questions and you go deeper, right, that tonality really starts to matter. But every question you ask has its own best tonality. So now when you go through the quizzes, right now when you go through the quizzes here, I want you to make sure that you match up all the right tonalities. I'm gonna get lots of examples here and I wanna make sure you lock this stuff in because it's crucial succeeding with straight line prospecting, all right? So go take your quiz down and I'll see you on the other side.
